citations.narrative:
The licensee is exhibiting fennec foxes at the facility and spider monkeys on the facility's website and social media. The
foxes are currently used in animal encounters with the public and the spider monkeys are to be used in animal encounters
in the near future. The licensee does not have a USDA license that authorizes the use of exotic and/or wild canids (which
includes foxes) or Group 6 non-human primates (which includes brachiating species such as spider monkeys). Failure to
acquire authorization for specific groups of animals does not ensure that the facility is knowledgeable and capable of
maintaining compliance with the care, husbandry, housing, and handling of these species.
Licenses authorize the use of animals subject to subparts A through F of the Animal Welfare Act regulations. Licenses
must specifically authorize the use of brachiating species of non-human primates and wild and/or exotic canids, and a
licensee must obtain a new license before using any animal beyond those types or numbers of animals authorized under
the existing license. The licensee must obtain a new licensee authorizing the use of fennec foxes and spider monkeys for
regulated purposes.
Correction date: Beginning April 2, 2026, the licensee must only exhibit animal species in which he has been authorized
for.

In the enclosure housing 6 llamas and 3 alpacas there are several wooden shelter structures with roofs constructed with
sheet metal. On one shelter all four corners of the metal roof have broken and/or bent upward creating sharp points at the
level of the animals.
There are numerous broken wires located on the outside of the bottom of the cygnet and fennec fox enclosures. The
broken wires are creating sharp points that are located at the level of 4 sheep and 6 goats.
One broken fence post is lying in the enclosures with 3 calves. The fence post is broken into 3 pieces, and one piece has
a large chunk of concrete attached to it.
Sharp points and broken fencing could cause injury to the animals.
Outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from
injury. The licensee must remove the broken metal roofing, broken wires, and broken fence post or alter them in a manner
that eliminates the risk of injury to the animals. The licensee must ensure the housing facilities remain in good repair at all
times.
To be corrected by: At the time of the inspection the broken wires, broken fence post, and metal roofing were repaired or
removed from the enclosures.

There is not a 6-foot perimeter fence around the fennec fox and sloth enclosures, instead, the licensee has attached large
pieces of plexiglass to the primary enclosure fencing to stop the public from placing their fingers inside the cages and to
stop unwanted animals from entering the enclosures. There is no written approval from the Administrator to use the
plexiglass walls in place of the 6-foot perimeter fence. A compliant perimeter fence is necessary to ensure that animals
and unauthorized persons do not come into contact with the regulated animals which could have a negative impact on
their health and well-being.
Outdoor housing facilities for non-dangerous animals must be enclosed by a 6-foot perimeter fence or must have primary
enclosure fencing made of sturdy, durable material, which may include certain types of concrete, wood, plastic, metal, or
glass and must be approved in writing by the Administrator. The licensee must either surround the fox and sloth
enclosures with a 6-foot perimeter fence or have written approval from the Administrator for the plexiglass walls to be
used in place of a perimeter fence.
To be corrected by: By April 8, 2026, the licensee must either request approval from the Administrator for the plexiglass
walls or enclose the sloths and foxes with a 6-foot perimeter fence.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Richard Burdeaux, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.1(b)(2)(ii) 
   
Requirements and application 
The licensee is exhibiting fennec foxes at the facility and spider monkeys on the facility's website and social media. The 
foxes are currently used in animal encounters with the public and the spider monkeys are to be used in animal encounters 
in the near future. The licensee does not have a USDA license that authorizes the use of exotic and/or wild canids (which

maintaining compliance with the care, husbandry, housing, and handling of these species.  
 
Licenses authorize the use of animals subject to subparts A through F of the Animal Welfare Act regulations. Licenses

licensee must obtain a new license before using any animal beyond those types or numbers of animals authorized under

Correction date: Beginning April 2, 2026, the licensee must only exhibit animal species in which he has been authorized

3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
In the enclosure housing 6 llamas and 3 alpacas there are several wooden shelter structures with roofs constructed with

There are numerous broken wires located on the outside of the bottom of the cygnet and fennec fox enclosures. The

One broken fence post is lying in the enclosures with 3 calves. The fence post is broken into 3 pieces, and one piece has 
a large chunk of concrete attached to it.  
 
Sharp points and broken fencing could cause injury to the animals.

Outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from 
injury. The licensee must remove the broken metal roofing, broken wires, and broken fence post or alter them in a manner

To be corrected by: At the time of the inspection the broken wires, broken fence post, and metal roofing were repaired or 
removed from the enclosures. 
3.127(d)(1) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
There is not a 6-foot perimeter fence around the fennec fox and sloth enclosures, instead, the licensee has attached large 
pieces of plexiglass to the primary enclosure fencing to stop the public from placing their fingers inside the cages and to 
stop unwanted animals from entering the enclosures. There is no written approval from the Administrator to use the

and unauthorized persons do not come into contact with the regulated animals which could have a negative impact on 
their health and well-being. 
 
Outdoor housing facilities for non-dangerous animals must be enclosed by a 6-foot perimeter fence or must have primary 
enclosure fencing made of sturdy, durable material, which may include certain types of concrete, wood, plastic, metal, or 
glass and must be approved in writing by the Administrator. The licensee must either surround the fox and sloth 
enclosures with a 6-foot perimeter fence or have written approval from the Administrator for the plexiglass walls to be 
used in place of a perimeter fence. 
 
To be corrected by: By April 8, 2026, the licensee must either request approval from the Administrator for the plexiglass 
walls or enclose the sloths and foxes with a 6-foot perimeter fence. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Richard Burdeaux, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

species.scientific: Ara, Anodorhynchus, Primolius, Diopsittaca spp hybrid

species.common: DOMESTIC PIG / POTBELLY PIG / MICRO PIG
